subsections.title: Stream Fundamentals
subsections.keyRule: Stream is a pipeline of operations on a sequence of elements. NOT a data structure — elements are consumed, not stored. Lazy — nothing executes until a terminal operation. Three parts: source → intermediate ops (filter/map/sorted) → terminal op (collect/forEach/count). Stream can only be consumed ONCE.

terms.what: A Stream pipeline = data source + zero or more intermediate operations + one terminal operation. Think assembly line — data flows through, each stage transforms it.
terms.why: Streams replace verbose for-loops with declarative, readable pipelines. Operations compose naturally without intermediate collections.
terms.where: Any collection processing, data transformation, aggregation, search, grouping.
terms.when: Use Streams for collection operations. Use traditional loops when: you need to break early with exact index, or when mutable state inside the loop is needed.
terms.issue: Calling stream() on the same Stream reference twice — throws IllegalStateException (stream already consumed).
terms.fix: Never reuse a Stream. Call .stream() again from the source collection if you need to process again.

star.s: Interview: what is a Stream and how is it lazy?
star.a: Stream is a pipeline. Lazy means intermediate operations do nothing until a terminal op is called — filter/map don't execute until collect/forEach triggers the pipeline.

terms.what: filter() keeps elements that match a Predicate. map() transforms each element using a Function, changing its type or value.
terms.why: The most common Stream operations. filter() reduces the dataset. map() transforms it.
terms.how: filter(e -> e.isActive()) keeps only active employees. map(Employee::getName) converts Employee to String name. Chain: filter then map.
terms.where: Every data processing pipeline. REST responses, search results, report generation.
terms.when: Use filter() before map() for efficiency — filter first reduces elements, then map() processes fewer elements.
terms.issue: Using map() to produce a Stream inside a Stream creates Stream<Stream<T>> — nested streams. Hard to process.
terms.fix: Use flatMap() to flatten nested streams: list.stream().flatMap(order -> order.getItems().stream()) — gives all items from all orders in one flat stream.

star.s: Get all product names from all orders.
star.r: Flat list of unique product names.

terms.what: collect() is a terminal operation that gathers Stream elements into a result — a List, Set, Map, String, or any custom container.
terms.why: Streams produce nothing until collected. collect() is how you get your result.
terms.how: toList(), toSet(), toMap(keyFn, valueFn), joining(', '), groupingBy(fn), counting(), summingInt(fn), partitioningBy(pred).
terms.where: End of every Stream pipeline that needs a collection result.
terms.when: Use toList() for most cases. groupingBy() for SQL GROUP BY equivalent. joining() for CSV/string output. counting() for frequency maps.
terms.issue: toMap() throws IllegalStateException if two keys map to the same value — duplicate key conflict.
terms.fix: Use toMap(keyFn, valueFn, mergeFunction) — third parameter resolves duplicates: (v1,v2) -> v1 keeps first, (v1,v2) -> v1+v2 merges.

terms.what: reduce() combines all elements into a single result using a BinaryOperator. count() returns number of elements. Both are terminal operations.
terms.why: reduce() is the building block for aggregations — sum, product, max, min. count() is the simplest aggregation.
terms.how: stream.reduce(0, Integer::sum) → sum of all integers. stream.count() → number. stream.mapToInt(Employee::getSalary).sum() — specialized numeric stream is faster.
terms.where: Totals, aggregations, finding max/min, concatenating strings.
terms.when: Use specialized streams (IntStream, LongStream) for numeric operations — mapToInt().sum() is faster than reduce(0,Integer::sum) because no boxing.
terms.issue: reduce() on an empty stream without identity throws NoSuchElementException if using the single-argument version.
terms.fix: Use reduce(identity, accumulator) — provide a starting value (0 for sum, 1 for product). Returns the identity if stream is empty.

terms.what: Optional is a container that either holds a value or is empty. It is a better return type than null for 'value might not exist'.
terms.why: Null returns cause NullPointerExceptions. Optional forces callers to acknowledge the possibility of no value — prevents NPE.
terms.how: Optional<User> user = repo.findById(id); user.isPresent(), user.get(), user.orElse(defaultUser), user.orElseGet(supplier), user.orElseThrow(), user.map(User::getName).
terms.where: Repository findById(), search operations, config lookups, any method that might not find a value.
terms.when: Use Optional as return type from methods. Do NOT use as field type or method parameter — poor performance and semantics.
terms.issue: Optional.get() on an empty Optional throws NoSuchElementException. Calling get() without isPresent() check is the same as unguarded null dereference.
terms.fix: Never use optional.get() without checking. Use orElse(), orElseGet(), orElseThrow(), or map() instead. These are safe chaining methods. 📌 Note: Optional.of(null) throws NullPointerException. Use Optional.ofNullable(value) when value might be null.

star.s: Service returns null for missing user. Callers get NPE.
star.a: Changed return type to Optional<User>. Callers use orElseThrow(UserNotFoundException::new).
star.r: Explicit contract — callers must handle absent case.

terms.what: Parallel streams split data into chunks and process them on multiple CPU threads simultaneously using the ForkJoinPool.
terms.why: For CPU-intensive operations on large datasets, parallelism can dramatically reduce processing time.
terms.how: list.parallelStream() or stream.parallel(). Internally uses ForkJoinPool.commonPool() with threads = number of CPUs. Merge results at end.
terms.where: Large dataset processing: batch transformations, statistical calculations, image processing.
terms.when: Use parallel streams when: data is large (10,000+ elements), operation is CPU-intensive (not I/O), order does not matter, operations are stateless and independent.
terms.issue: Parallel streams on small datasets are SLOWER than sequential — thread coordination overhead exceeds savings. Also, shared mutable state in parallel stream = race conditions.
terms.fix: Benchmark before using parallel. Never use stateful operations in parallel streams. For I/O- bound tasks, use CompletableFuture with custom executor instead.

star.s: 1 million record price calculation taking 8 seconds.
star.a: Changed stream() to parallelStream(). Stateless operations.

subsections.title: Stream Creation Methods
subsections.keyRule: Create streams from: Collection.stream(), Arrays.stream(), Stream.of(), Stream.generate() (infinite), Stream.iterate() (infinite), IntStream.range() (int ranges). Infinite streams need limit() or takeWhile() to terminate.

terms.what: Stream.of(a,b,c) creates a stream from explicit values. Arrays.stream(arr) creates a stream from an array. Both create finite sequential streams.
terms.why: Not all data lives in Collections. You may need a stream from a few values or from an array.
terms.how: Stream<String> s = Stream.of('a','b','c'); Stream<Integer> nums = Arrays.stream(new int[]{1,2,3}).boxed(); IntStream ints = Arrays.stream(new int[]{1,2,3});
terms.where: Test data, combining small sets of known values, converting arrays to streams.
terms.when: Use Stream.of() for small literal sets. Use Arrays.stream() for converting arrays to streams.
terms.issue: Stream.of(array) gives Stream<int[]> for int[] — not Stream<Integer>. Must use Arrays.stream(intArray).
terms.fix: For primitive arrays: always use Arrays.stream(intArray) to get IntStream. Use .boxed() to convert to Stream<Integer>.

star.s: Need to process int[] as stream.
star.r: Correct stream type. No boxing mistakes.

terms.what: Stream.generate(Supplier) creates an infinite stream from a Supplier. Stream.iterate(seed, UnaryOperator) creates an infinite stream starting from seed, each element derived from previous.
terms.why: For creating infinite sequences: Fibonacci, UUIDs, random numbers, counter sequences.
terms.how: Stream.generate(UUID::randomUUID).limit(5) — 5 random UUIDs. Stream.iterate(0, n->n+1).limit(10) — 0 to 9. Java 9: Stream.iterate(0, n<100, n->n+1) — with predicate to stop.
terms.where: Random data generation, sequence generation, simulation, test data factories.
terms.when: Use generate() for stateless generation (each element independent). Use iterate() for stateful sequences (each depends on previous).
terms.issue: Infinite stream without limit() or takeWhile() runs forever — hangs the program.
terms.fix: Always add .limit(n) or .takeWhile(predicate) to infinite streams.

star.s: Need Fibonacci sequence as a stream.
star.a: Stream.iterate(new long[]{0,1}, f -> new long[]{f[1], f[0]+f[1]}).map(f -> f[0]).limit(20).

terms.what: IntStream.range(start, end) creates an int stream from start (inclusive) to end (exclusive). rangeClosed(start, end) includes end.
terms.why: Common need: iterate over index ranges, sum ranges, generate numeric sequences — without boxing to Integer.
terms.where: Index-based stream operations, sum/average over ranges, replace traditional for(int i=0; i<n; i++) loops.
terms.when: Use IntStream.range() instead of a for loop when you want functional-style index iteration.
terms.issue: IntStream.range() is half-open [start, end). Forgetting this causes off-by-one errors.

subsections.title: Stream Operations Extended
subsections.keyRule: peek() = debug without modifying. anyMatch/allMatch/noneMatch = short-circuit boolean terminals. mapToInt/Long/Double = switch to primitive stream, avoid boxing. Collectors.partitioningBy = split into two groups (true/false). Collectors.counting = count per group.

terms.what: peek() is an intermediate operation that performs an action on each element as it passes through the stream, without modifying the element. For debugging.
terms.why: Lets you inspect stream elements at any point in the pipeline without breaking the chain.
terms.where: Debugging stream pipelines, logging transformation steps, performance tracing.
terms.when: Use peek() during development for debugging. Remove or comment out in production — peek() inside a lazy stream may not execute as expected.
terms.issue: peek() with side effects in production can be unreliable — lazy evaluation and short-circuit operations may skip some elements.
terms.fix: For production logging, use a map() that logs and returns the original element. peek() is best kept in development/debug code.

star.s: Stream pipeline produces wrong output — hard to trace where.
star.a: Added .peek(e -> log.debug('element: {}', e)) between each step. Found filter removing too many elements. Fixed predicate.

terms.what: anyMatch(predicate): true if any element matches. allMatch(predicate): true if all match. noneMatch(predicate): true if none match. All are short-circuit terminal operations.
terms.why: Check collection membership or conditions without iterating the entire stream — short-circuits as soon as answer is known.
terms.where: Validation checks, membership tests, condition verifications on collections.
terms.when: Use instead of filter().count() > 0 or filter().findFirst().isPresent() — these are clearer and more efficient.
terms.issue: allMatch() and noneMatch() return true for empty streams — 'vacuous truth'. This surprises beginners.
terms.fix: If empty stream is possible and would change your logic, add an isEmpty check before streaming.

star.s: Validate that all items in order are in stock.

terms.what: mapToInt/Long/Double converts a Stream<T> to an IntStream/LongStream/DoubleStream — primitive streams that support sum(), average(), min(), max() directly.
terms.why: Stream<Integer> cannot call .sum() directly. mapToInt converts to IntStream which has sum(), average(), range statistics — and avoids boxing. TERM MEANING
terms.where: Summing, averaging, finding min/max of numeric fields in a collection of objects.
terms.when: Use mapToInt/Long/Double whenever doing numeric aggregation — avoids boxing overhead and adds numeric methods.
terms.issue: Not using mapToInt for numeric operations means using reduce(0, Integer::sum) which boxes everything.
terms.fix: Replace stream.map(o -> o.getCount()).reduce(0, Integer::sum) with stream.mapToInt(o -> o.getCount()).sum() — cleaner and faster.

star.s: Sum all order amounts in a stream of Order objects.
star.r: No boxing, direct sum, clean API.

terms.what: Spliterator (Splittable Iterator) is used by streams internally for parallel splitting. It can split a source into two parts for parallel processing.
terms.why: Parallel streams need to divide work. Spliterator knows how to split the data source (List, array, etc.) into independent chunks.
terms.how: You rarely use Spliterator directly. Internally: Stream.parallel() asks source's Spliterator to split. ForkJoinPool processes each part. merge results.
terms.where: Custom data source for streams, understanding parallel stream internals, creating custom stream sources.
terms.when: Implement Spliterator when creating a custom stream source. Otherwise it's internal to the stream API.
terms.issue: Not marking Spliterator as SIZED or ORDERED correctly leads to suboptimal parallel performance.
terms.fix: For custom Spliterator, set characteristics correctly (SIZED, SUBSIZED, ORDERED, SORTED, DISTINCT, IMMUTABLE).

star.s: Interview: how does parallel stream split work?
star.a: Each Collection provides a Spliterator. Parallel stream calls trySplit() recursively. ForkJoinPool workers process each split. Results merged.

subsections.title: Optional Deep Dive
subsections.keyRule: Optional.of(value) — value must not be null. Optional.ofNullable(value) — null-safe. Optional.empty() — explicitly empty. Check with isPresent()/isEmpty(). Transform with

terms.what: Optional.of(v) wraps a non-null value — throws NPE if null. Optional.ofNullable(v) wraps value OR empty if null. Optional.empty() explicitly represents no value.
terms.why: Precise Optional creation: of() documents 'this must not be null', ofNullable() handles uncertain values.
terms.how: Optional<String> safe = Optional.ofNullable(maybeNull); Optional<String> definite = Optional.of(notNull); Optional<String> none = Optional.empty();
terms.where: Method return types, wrapper for nullable results, combining with stream filter.
terms.when: Use ofNullable() when wrapping values that might be null. Use of() when you know the value is never null (documents intent). Use empty() to return 'no result' explicitly.
terms.issue: Optional.of(null) throws NullPointerException immediately — not the intent of using Optional.
terms.fix: Use Optional.ofNullable() when the value might be null. Use Optional.of() only when you are 100% sure the value is non-null.

terms.what: isPresent() returns true if Optional has a value. isEmpty() (Java 11+) returns true if Optional is empty. Both check if value exists.
terms.why: Before using the value, you must check if it's there.
terms.how: Optional<User> user = findUser(id); if(user.isPresent()) { process(user.get()); } // Java 11+: if(user.isEmpty()) { return notFound; }
terms.where: Any Optional usage before calling get(). Though prefer ifPresent() / orElse() over isPresent() + get().
terms.when: Use isPresent()/isEmpty() when you need to branch on presence. For transformations, prefer map/flatMap which handle empty automatically.
terms.issue: Anti-pattern: optional.isPresent() ? optional.get() : default — defeats Optional purpose. Same as null check.
terms.fix: Use optional.orElse(default) instead. Even better: optional.map(transform).orElse(default).

star.s: Code: if(opt.isPresent()) { return opt.get().getName(); } else { return 'unknown'; }

terms.what: ifPresent(consumer) runs the consumer if value present — ignores if empty. ifPresentOrElse(consumer, emptyAction) (Java 9+) runs consumer if present, emptyAction if empty.
terms.why: Cleaner alternative to isPresent() + get() for side effects — no chance of forgetting get() or calling get() when empty.
terms.how: opt.ifPresent(user -> log.info('Found: {}', user.getName())); opt.ifPresentOrElse(user -> sendEmail(user), () -> log.warn('User not found'));
terms.where: Logging, sending notifications, caching — side effects on optional values.
terms.when: Use ifPresent() for single side effects. Use ifPresentOrElse() when you need to react to both cases.
terms.issue: ifPresent() is void — cannot be used in method chains for transformations.
terms.fix: For transformations, use map()/flatMap(). For side effects only, use ifPresent()/ifPresentOrElse().

star.s: Need to log when user found and warn when not.
star.a: opt.ifPresentOrElse(u -> log.info('User found: {}', u), () -> log.warn('User not found')).

terms.what: flatMap(f) applies a function that returns Optional and flattens — avoids Optional<Optional<T>>. filter(predicate) returns empty Optional if value doesn't match.
orElse(value) always evaluates the fallback. orElseGet(Supplier) evaluates lazily — only if empty. orElseThrow(Supplier) throws the given exception if empty.
terms.why: orElse(expensiveCall()) always calls expensiveCall, even when value is present — wasteful. orElseGet() only calls the supplier when needed.
terms.how: String name = opt.orElse('Unknown'); // always creates 'Unknown' string. String name = opt.orElseGet(this::generateDefault); // only called if empty. String name = opt.orElseThrow(() -> new NotFoundException(id));
terms.where: Default values, fallback computation, mandatory values where absence is an error.
terms.when: Use orElse() for cheap constant defaults. Use orElseGet() for expensive computations. Use orElseThrow() for mandatory values.
terms.issue: opt.orElse(expensiveDBCall()) — DB call happens even when Optional has a value. Wastes resources.
terms.fix: opt.orElseGet(() -> expensiveDBCall()) — DB call only when Optional is empty.

star.s: Performance issue: DB fallback called even when cache hits.
star.r: DB called only on cache miss. Performance improved.
